data.title: Politics And The Markets 09/19/25
data.description:
This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

More on Today's Markets:

Moderation Guidelines:

We remove comments under the following categories:

  • Personal attacks on another user account
  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation
  • Stereotyping, prejudiced or racist language about individuals or the topic under discussion.
  • Inciting violence messages, encouraging hate groups and political violence.

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

For any issue with regards to comments please email us at : moderation@seekingalpha.com.

Seeking Alpha's Disclosure: Past performance is no guarantee of future results. No recommendation or advice is being given as to whether any investment is suitable for a particular investor. Any views or opinions expressed above may not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker or US investment adviser or investment bank. Our analysts are third party authors that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body.

data.title: Nokia: Increasingly Attractive With New AI Partnerships
data.description:
At the beginning of this year, I became one of the first analysts to issue a Buy recommendation for Nokia (NYSE:NOK). Since then, the stock has been highly volatile, jumping 19% from January to June and then dropping sharply back to the same level as at the beginning of the year.

The fluctuations in the stock price are somewhat understandable, as Nokia continues to struggle to return to proper growth. The company also recently lowered its guidance for the rest of the year after Q2 results. Still, Nokia shows many significant but less noticeable improvements in its AI-driven business, and with new partnerships, the company might surprise the market in the coming years. Therefore, due to NOK's low valuation and high potential upside, the stock remains a cautious Buy with a solid risk/reward profile.

Nokia's partnerships, including with Super Micro Computer, are encouraging

One of the biggest positives I reviewed in my previous articles on Nokia was the company's rapidly growing list of partnerships and new contracts. At the time, NOK's page on Seeking Alpha seemed to be full of news about Nokia's contract wins in the areas of AI and 5G with companies like Microsoft (MSFT) and Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF). These were encouraging additions to already existing partners like Dell (DELL), Nvidia (NVDA), and Google (GOOG). Now, about nine months after my previous article, there is even more positive news in that area.

One of the most interesting developments is the recently announced collaboration between Nokia and Super Micro (SMCI). Nokia's tech will allow Supermicro to deploy "plug-and-play" data center solutions optimized for AI, including all the necessary networking equipment and software. In other words, Supermicro's packaged hardware becomes even easier to design, install, and use for end customers like cloud providers or hyperscalers, since they would not need to think about communication and networking technologies separately.

The combined solution includes Supermicro's 800G Ethernet switching platforms integrated with Nokia's Service Router Linux (SR Linux) Network Operating System (NOS) and Nokia's Event-Driven Automation (EDA) as the infrastructure platform to automate the entire data center network lifecycle from Day 0 design, Day 1 deployment to Day 2+ daily operations.

The deal should be highly beneficial for both sides. Supermicro's offerings become even more competitive thanks to the tight integration with Nokia's networking and automation technologies, improving the value proposition of its packaged data center products. For more details on how the deal affects SMCI, there is a solid roundup by Weebler Finance.

In turn, Nokia relatively "easily" gains even more exposure to the AI data center market. The company's tech has already been used for this application for some time. For example, last year, Nokia and Microsoft expanded their multi-year agreement to supply Azure data center networks. The deal with Supermicro is a great addition to Nokia's AI portfolio as the company does not need to invest any additional money in sales and marketing - Supermicro will handle sales and promotion, while Nokia's tech will just come prepackaged with SMCI's racks.

Supermicro has had tough times lately, from slowing revenue growth to financial investigations. Still, the company is expected to generate almost $32 billion in revenue in the coming financial year, which is almost 50% higher than Nokia's total revenue projected for 2025. Therefore, even a small share of SMCI's sales might boost Nokia's financials substantially and help the company beat modest sales estimates. (the market estimates just a 3.9% revenue growth for NOK next year)

Maybe even more importantly, the partnership with Supermicro might become a strong signal to the rest of the industry to implement Nokia's tech in the AI data center space. If Nokia proves its expertise successfully, the company might capture a bigger share of the general AI data center market, which is forecast to grow at a 31.6% CAGR over the next five years, reaching almost $934 billion by 2030. Again, since Nokia has a relatively small revenue baseline of about $23 billion in 2025, additional AI partnerships could be a significant catalyst for growth.

Beyond this, Nokia has continued to score more partnerships and collaborations since my previous article on the company. Some of the bigger and more interesting ones include:

  • Lockheed Martin (LMT), which will integrate Nokia's tech into its military-grade 5G network
  • A patent agreement with Amazon (AMZN), which uses Nokia's video technology for its streaming services
  • A cybersecurity-focused deal with Fibrus to deploy the Nokia Deepfield solution across its network
  • Indosat, which will use Nokia's AI-driven energy management solution in its networks
  • A recent collaboration with Kongsberg focused on deployable 5G solutions and future 6G solutions in the defense sector

and several others.

With this solid and relatively diversified portfolio, Nokia can expand its revenue potential over the coming years. This is especially encouraging because AI and increasing defense spending around the world will likely require significant network expansions, so Nokia might experience a growth supercycle at some point when all the partnerships fully kick in.

Nokia's financials remain healthy, even if not impressive

Even before all the new partnerships, Nokia has finally started to show the first signs of a turnaround in the latest quarter after a long period of declining revenues. In the second quarter of 2025, the company generated €4.5 billion in sales, about 2% higher on a reported basis than in FQ2 2024. This might not seem like a significant improvement, but these results were achieved despite significant currency and tariff headwinds the company experienced during the period.

The company also demonstrated solid financial performance in the first half of 2025, with €800 million in free cash flows, about €6 billion in total cash at the end of Q2, and about €3 billion in debt. (long-term borrowings plus current portion of debt). Therefore, Nokia should not face any significant financial risks in the medium term.

More important, in my view, are the results by segment. Nokia's biggest segment, Network Infrastructure, increased its revenue by 8% in constant currency, with the Optical Networks subsegment's sales surging by 80% year-over-year. This subsegment benefited the most from hyperscalers' demand in North America; in other words, the AI data center buildups started to boost Nokia's revenue substantially. Cloud and Network Services' revenue also increased by 14% year-over-year, thanks to strong demand for 5G.

To me, these more hidden developments are crucial because Nokia establishes itself as an essential company for AI infrastructure. The situation starts to look similar to what Corning (GLW) has experienced in the last year or so - the market had ignored the underlying developments in Corning's AI-driven segments for a long time until the company started demonstrating consistent double-digit revenue growth. Now, Corning's valuation has surged by 68% in the last six months alone. If Nokia maintains its momentum in Network Infrastructure and Cloud and keeps expanding its aforementioned partnership list, the company's results can easily surprise the market in the coming years. After that, NOK's valuation might start surging quickly, similar to GLW.

Nokia's valuation is very low, especially in light of recent developments

Despite all the underlying improvements in Nokia's business and new partnerships, the stock's valuation remains relatively low. Its 2026 P/E is just under 12, which is slightly lower than that of the direct competitor Ericsson (ERIC) and more than two times lower than that of Corning. (Corning's business is very different from Nokia's, but its AI data center business has gone through similar developments to what Nokia is experiencing now.) NOK's forward EV/Sales is also under 1, which is unusual for a growing company, even if this growth is negligible at the moment. And because Nokia is profitable, this leads to an EV/EBITDA of just 7.2, about 2.6 times lower than that of GLW.

Clearly, Nokia still needs to solve a lot of issues and prove itself as an AI player. Still, with all the aforementioned partnerships, the company might return to a 10-20% average earnings growth in the coming years. (NOK's EPS is currently expected to grow 28.4% in 2026 and 10.7% in 2027) With a conservative PEG ratio of about 1.5, Nokia could then be valued at a forward P/E of 15-25, which results in a 2025 price target range of $5.85-9.75. It is unlikely the stock will hit the upper bound of the range any time soon unless Nokia's revenue accelerates faster than expected. Still, NOK's risk/reward ratio looks favorable here.

Risks and key takeaways

While my analysis is based on a moderately optimistic long-term view of the company, Nokia still faces risks in the short term. Firstly, the company's Mobile Networks segment remains highly volatile, and its revenue already declined 13% in Q2 on a constant currency basis. This segment was the second-largest by revenue in Q2. Secondly, Nokia faces significant currency and tariff headwinds, which negatively impacted Q2 results. Finally, even though Nokia generates positive free cash flows, the company's margins are too low for the current market, with a comparable operating margin of just 5.1% in Q1-Q2 2025. (I explored this topic in my previous article in more detail)

Despite these risks and negatives, I believe NOK remains attractive for a long-term investment. The company's exposure to the AI data center market could lead to significant revenue expansion in the coming years. This becomes more likely with new partnerships, like the recently announced collaboration with Super Micro Computer. Meanwhile, Nokia's financial performance remains healthy, with positive free cash flow, manageable debt, and a positive net cash position. With low valuation multiples, NOK still presents a solid risk/reward opportunity. Therefore, I maintain my Buy rating.

data.title: Restaurant Brands International: Cyclical Weakness Opens Up A Decent Entry Point
data.description:
Like many consumer-facing stocks, Restaurant Brands International (NYSE:QSR)(TSX:QSR:CA)('RBI') has been a relatively weak performer in recent quarters. Including dividends, shares of the Burger King and Tim Hortons owner have declined by around 6% since my last update back in November - a performance that is around 17 points worse than similarly-timed investments in both the S&P 500 and the broader consumer discretionary space.

Looking back, cyclical softness was my main concern when I last covered the company. A then-valuation of around 21x EPS didn't seem excessive given its growth prospects and asset-light business model, but with comparable sales growth easing, a "Hold" rating seemed prudent.

Though Q2 showed welcome signs of improvement, underlying growth continues to undershoot management's longer-term goal, while RBI is also experiencing another cycle-related headwind from higher beef prices. While these issues are weighing on the firm right now, I do think this is reflected in today's cheaper valuation. Accordingly, I am shifting my rating to "Buy".

Growth Algorithm Recap

RBI has just over 32,000 restaurants in its system. These consist of the hamburger-themed Burger King brand (~60% of its stores), Canadian coffee chain Tim Hortons (~20%), fried chicken concept Popeyes Louisiana Kitchen (~15%), and sandwich shop Firehouse Subs (~4%).

Many readers will know that RBI is largely a franchisor rather than an owner-operator. Only ~5% of its restaurants are operated by the firm directly. Since franchise revenue typically consists of a mid-single-digit cut of restaurant-level sales, RBI is shielded to an extent from short-term movements in restaurant-level profitability.

It also means that system-wide sales is a major KPI, as this will ultimately drive franchise fees and earnings. In turn, system-wide sales growth is a function of comparable-store sales growth and store count growth. With that in mind, management's medium-term growth algorithm is relatively simple: grow comparable sales by 3% per year and the store count by 5%, giving 8% annualized growth in system-wide sales and operating profit.

Facing Cyclical Headwinds

The macro environment does appear to be weighing on these levers right now. As you can see in the chart below, trends in U.S. consumer confidence haven't exactly been bullish since my last piece. Granted, RBI is geographically diverse, but the U.S. market is still key, accounting for over one-third of its total restaurants and an even larger share of system-wide sales.

With that, RBI's comparable sales grew just 1.3% over the first six months of the year. That was around 190 basis points lower than the year-before period and around 170 basis points below management's 3% goal. The U.S. market was particularly soft, with comps at Burger King (+0.2%), Popeyes (-2.4%) and Firehouse Subs (-0.4%) all relatively soft there. In contrast, the firm's international segment reported 3.4% comparable sales growth in H1.

Unit count is also lagging a little. Net restaurant growth came in at 2.9% in the first half of the year, down around 110 basis points year-on-year and 210 basis points below management's goal. As a result, systemwide sales grew by just 4.1%, down around 240 basis points year-on-year.

At the same time, the company is also seeing some headwinds from commodity prices, specifically beef, which is obviously a key input at Burger King. As well as affecting franchisee-level profitability, this is also weighing on its own company-operated stores, with Burger King restaurant-level EBITDA margin falling 120 basis points year-on-year last quarter to 12.1%.

There isn't much that management can do about the business and commodity cycle, and the firm wasn't alone in seeing weak comps in H1. Peers like McDonald's (MCD) and Wendy's (WEN) were all in the same boat to varying degrees.

Some Positives To Take Away

The above in mind, the current soft performance versus management's long-term algorithm isn't a major concern of mine. Depending on where we are in the business cycle, results will be above-average in some years and below-average in others.

While the latter is the case right now, there were also some positives to take away from the company's first-half results. For instance, there was a big sequential improvement in the second quarter, with comps up 2.4%. This was around 230 basis points higher than Q1. At 5.3%, system sales growth was also healthier, up 250 basis points quarter-on-quarter.

Also, international growth continues to outperform. This is important because the firm's non-U.S. footprint is the key driver of its long-term growth prospects. The explanation for this lies in the make up of the firm's domestic footprint, where over 50% of the company's restaurants are Burger Kings, which is in structural decline in the U.S. due to mediocre unit-level profitability. Unpacking that, you can see in the image below that annual franchisee-level EBITDA was $205,000 for domestic Burger King stores in 2023 and 2024. The investment required to bring one Burger King unit online varies heavily depending on the exact type of restaurant but, on average, a figure of roughly $2 million has been suggested.

This would imply a cash-on-cash of return of around 10%. With modest economics, franchisees have little incentive to open up new units. Indeed, RBI reported 6,659 U.S. Burger King outlets at the end of last quarter. This was down around 1.6% year-on-year and is 8% lower than five years ago. Management is trying to turn things around, targeting ~$300,000 in average annual franchisee-level EBITDA in the medium-term, but time will tell if this will stabilize the brand.

Burger King is a much healthier brand abroad, as evidenced by its ongoing growth. There were 13,007 Burger King stores outside the U.S. at the end of Q2, up 2.6% year-on-year and around 13% higher than five years ago. Comparable sales were also solid for the brand, up 3.4% in H1 outside the U.S. (4.1% in Q2). This is helping power solid non-U.S. growth. In total, RBI's international segment posted 3.4% comparable sales growth in H1 and over 5% net restaurant growth. System sales grew by over 9%.

The Valuation

As a result of hitting a cyclical soft patch, the stock is down around 9% in price terms since my last piece, with the shares currently trading for around $62.95 each. Meanwhile, earnings have gone in the opposite direction. The company made $1.70 per share in adjusted net income in the first half, over 6% higher than in H1 2024.

The contrast between its share price and earnings has seen the valuation contract. Like I said in the introduction, RBI was trading for around 21x consensus EPS when I last covered it. Today, shares trade for around 17.5x consensus 2025 EPS.

That puts today's buyer in a slightly stronger position. Indeed, powered by its brighter international prospects, analysts still expect the firm to grow EPS at a high single-digit annualized clip in the next few years.

On top of that, the asset-light nature of its franchised business model means that RBI should earn very high returns on incremental investment. Per the Q2 earnings call, management is guiding for just $400-$450 million in capital expenditures and cash inducements this year, equal to just ~1% of its annual system-wide sales. This feature of its business allows RBI to spend heavily on shareholder distributions instead. The company currently pays a $0.62 per share quarterly dividend, for a circa 70% payout ratio. Annualized, its dividend yield is now nearly 4%.

Between high single-digit annualized EPS growth and a circa 4% dividend yield, the company is offering around 12%-13% annual returns from its business. With the P/E ratio at 17.5x, multiple contraction is going to be less of a long-term headwind than it was at 21x, but even so, today's buyer could still afford to see some compression while walking away with a 10%-plus annualized return. Accordingly, I am upgrading the shares to "Buy".

data.title: Blue Owl Capital: Still A Solid BDC But NAV Is Trending Down
data.description:
Overview

Blue Owl Capital Corporation (NYSE:OBDC) operates as a business development company that aims to generate its earnings through a diverse portfolio of debt investments. As the name implies, OBDC lends capital to businesses to help develop and expand operations. In return, OBDC relies on interest or equity that it receives from these borrowers. When I previously covered OBDC, I initiated a downgrade to a hold due to weaker earnings. OBDC reported its Q2 earnings last month, so I wanted to revisit this BDC to provide a more up-to-date look at its performance and outlook for the remainder of the year.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that OBDC's share price has declined by about 6.8%. The most recent decline can likely be attributed to the latest earnings report. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return sits around 3.85% over the same time frame. Following the recent price decline, OBDC now offers investors a generous starting dividend yield of 11.7%, which makes it an enticing choice for investors seeking a high level of income generation. Although earnings have declined from their prior levels, dividend coverage still remains solid.

Following the recent price decline, OBDC now trades at an attractive discount to NAV valuation. While this may present an opportunity for long-term investors to accumulate shares, it can also be a sign of the recent weakness that OBDC has demonstrated. The internal health of the portfolio remains strong, but there are definitely areas where management can improve. I anticipate that the overall BDC environment will improve once interest rates are cut and borrowing activity increases. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying portfolio strategy that OBDC implements to generate its earnings.

Portfolio Strategy

According to the latest portfolio breakdown, OBDC now has investments at fair value of $16.8B that are spread across 233 different portfolio companies within. What I like about the portfolio is that 98% of the investments are structured on a floating rate basis. This has allowed OBDC to capitalize on the higher interest rate environment. A higher interest rate directly translates to a higher level of interest income that can be collected from portfolio companies. If management's underwriting remains strong, they will select portfolio companies that generate enough income to handle the elevated interest expense.

Additionally, OBDC prioritizes the first lien senior secured structure across the majority of portfolio companies. This is ideal because first lien debt sits at the top of the corporate capital structure and has the highest priority for repayment. This acts as a defensive measure for OBDC in a situation where a borrower is underperforming and needs to liquidate assets. In this scenario, OBDC would be entitled for repayment before other forms of debt, which decreases the probability of OBDC losing all invested capital. The portfolio's breakdown by debt structure is as follows:

  • First Lien Senior Secured: 76%
  • Common Equity: 11%
  • Second Lien Senior Secured: 5%
  • Preferred Equity: 3%
  • Unsecured 2%
  • Joint Ventures: 2%

As we can see, OBDC also maintains exposure to equity-structured investments, which sit at the bottom of the corporate capital structure and has the lowest priority for repayment. Equity investments may not produce the most consistent level of income, but it makes it possible for OBDC to participate in capital appreciation from the underlying growth of portfolio companies.

Lastly, OBDC maintains an efficient level of diversity across different industries. As we can see above, OBDC is most heavily allocated to Internet Software & Service investments at 10.9% of the portfolio. This is followed by exposure to Healthcare Providers and Food & Beverage investments, which account for 8.3% and 6.6% of the portfolio, respectively. The portfolio has a weighted average total yield of 10.1%.

Valuation

When I previously covered OBDC, it traded at a discount to NAV of 3.4%. Following the recent price decline, OBDC now trades at a discount to NAV of approximately 8.85%. For reference, OBDC has traded at an average discount to NAV of 6.79% over the last five year period. This growing discount can be an indication of some internal weakness of OBDC. To better conceptualize the current discount level, I added the red line to the graph below.

Wall Street analysts currently have an average price target of $15.68 per share, which indicates a potential upside of over 14.4% from the current level. However, I do not believe that OBDC currently has any catalysts to allow this sort of double-digit growth to happen. For instance, OBDC failed to deliver positive net investment activity over the last quarter. OBDC was able to secure $1.11B in gross originations / new investment commitments during the quarter. However, this was offset by sales and repayments of $1.9B during the quarter, leaving a negative net funded activity of $1B. As a result, OBDC's base assets have become smaller over the last quarter.

However, the anticipation of several interest rate cuts throughout the remainder of 2025 can serve as a short-term catalyst. For reference, interest rates still remain near their decade highs, which has made it costly for businesses to hold debt on their balance sheet. As a result, it has been challenging for BDCs to source new deals that can grow their base assets. A lower interest rate may temporarily lead to lower net investment income, but it provides the opportunity for increased borrowing activity. If borrowing activity increases, OBDC may be able to grow its base assets and expand earnings if management selects high quality deals.

Q2 Earnings & Risk Profile

OBDC reported its Q2 earnings in early August, and the results were solid, but still revealed some weaknesses. Most notably, adjusted net investment income amounted to $0.40 per share, which is still a sizeable decline from the prior year, but a slight improvement from last quarter. The slight uptick in earnings from the prior quarter can be attributed to a rise in interest income from investments. Total investment income landed at $480.5M, which was an increase from the prior quarter's investment income of $458.9M.

However, OBDC's total expenses have slightly increased to $266.8M. The rise in expenses can mostly be attributed to the combination of interest expenses, management fees, and performance-based incentive fees. Despite the rising fees, OBDC has been able to lower its debt-to-equity ratio down to 1.17x, which is a moderate improvement from the prior quarter's debt-to-equity ratio of 1.26x. A lower ratio simply indicates that OBDC has decreased their reliance on debt to fuel growth.

From a balance sheet perspective, OBDC is still looking healthy. For instance, total debt has decreased from $10.1B down to $9.2B in the last quarter. Additionally, OBDC still has $360M of cash and equivalents on hand, which may be allocated to new investment activity after interest rates are cut. Despite this, OBDC may need to continue to prioritize reducing its debt burden and increasing earnings to slow the NAV decline. For reference, here are the quarter-end NAV amounts:

  • Q2 2024: $15.36 per share
  • Q3 2024: $15.28 per share
  • Q4 2024: $15.26 per share
  • Q1 2025: $15.14 per share
  • Q2 2025: $15.03 per share

On the bright side, OBDC's portfolio still remains strong, and non-accruals are relatively low. Non-accruals represent the rate of portfolio companies that are materially underperforming and cannot keep up with their required debt maintenance. When a portfolio company gets put onto non-accrual status, it is no longer contributing towards a BDC's earnings, and it reduces the overall quality of the portfolio.

According to the most recent earnings call, OBDC's non-accruals now sit at 0.7% of fair portfolio value and 1.6% at cost. Non-accruals remain almost identical to last quarter's non-accrual rate of 0.8% at fair value and 1.4% at cost. This indicates that the portfolio continues to remain resilient through the higher interest rate environment.

Another important metric that I look for is the level of PIK (payment-in-kind) interest income. PIK interest income represents revenue that is collected through the issuance of additional debt or equity, rather than actual cash flow. It's a method that allows portfolio companies to defer payments until a later date when cash improves. So a growing PIK interest income can be a sign that portfolio companies are tight on cash and some deterioration may be happening. Thankfully, the latest Form 10-Q indicates that PIK interest income is now a smaller percentage of OBDC's overall investment income.

Dividend Sustainability

As of the latest declared quarterly distribution of $0.37 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 11.7%. As previously mentioned, adjusted net investment income for the quarter landed at $0.40 per share. This indicates that earnings support the dividend at a coverage rate of about 108%. While it's great that net investment income still supports the distributions, I typically like to see the coverage rate above 110%. A strong coverage rate alleviates any concerns about the possibility of a dividend cut.

I believe that the primary reason for investors to hold a high-yield asset like BDCs, is for the income they can provide. If that income is under threat of a reduction, it defeats the purpose of maintaining a position. However, management did confirm that they still have undistributed spillover income of $0.33 per share, which helps reduce some of my concerns. It seems like management wants to prioritize steady distributions for shareholders, so hopefully net investment income improves in the near-term.

Further, our spillover income remains healthy at approximately $0.33 per share and equates to nearly a full quarter's worth of base dividends. We believe having a meaningful undistributed spillover supports our goal of maintaining a steady dividend through volatile and varying market conditions. - Jonathan Lamm, CFO

It's worth noting that distributions received from OBDC are not tax-efficient since they are funded by the repayment of loans. Therefore, any income received from OBDC is likely to be classified as ordinary dividends, which have the greatest tax burden for investors. So based on your specific tax bracket, the amount of capital invested, and desired income level, a position in OBDC may be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account to reduce the overall tax consequences.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my hold rating on OBDC at this time. There was a slight uptick in net investment income, but the overall NAV per share has still declined. A lower interest rate environment may stimulate some growth by increasing borrowing activity. Therefore, I will continue to monitor the overall growth metrics going forward, such as how much capital management is allocating to new investment activity. I would also like to see the distribution coverage get stronger over the next twelve months. While I see no reasons to sell yet, I also do not believe it is the most ideal time to accumulate shares during the uncertainty.

data.title: Coastal Financial: Slashing The EPS Estimate And Downgrading To Sell
data.description:
I adopted a hold rating on Coastal Financial Corporation (NASDAQ:CCB) in my last report released in March. Since then, the stock price has shot up, but the company’s earnings have actually missed my expectations. Due to the earnings miss and a worsening of my outlook, I’m now slashing my estimates for both the non-interest income and the earnings per share. Following the recent market price rally and the downward earnings revision, I think it’s time to downgrade Coastal Financial to a sell rating.

CCBX’s Revenues Likely to Remain Depressed

Coastal Financial’s business model is a bit different from other bank holding companies. CCB’s revenue mix changes from quarter to quarter, but usually only about half of the revenue comes from conventional community banking activities. The remaining revenue comes from CCBX, which is the banking as a service (“BaaS”) division. Under CCBX, Coastal Financial originates credit card loans and short-term loans mostly for the purpose of reselling. CCBX also takes in checking and savings deposits for the customers of different partners, including some big names like Remitly, Robinhood, and T-Mobile.

Income from CCBX, which flows into the non-interest income line item of the income statement, fell in the first quarter and then fell some more in the second quarter of this year. The company reported non-interest income of $106 million in the first half of the year, down 32% year-over-year. The first half’s performance was much worse than my expectations given in my last report on the company.

For the second half of the year, I’m expecting this division to continue to remain dull. This is because the consumer sentiment continues to be quite low, which will keep consumers away from large purchases on credit.

Further, new credit card customer additions might slow down because young graduates are especially having a hard time finding their first jobs. As a result, fewer young people will get approved for their first credit cards. As shown below, the difference between the unemployment rate of young people and the overall population has widened in recent months.

Overall, I’m expecting the non-interest income to remain stable near the second quarter’s level for the second half of this year. I’m estimating a non-interest income of $192 million for 2025, down by a hefty 38% from last year’s level of $308 million. I’ve slashed my estimate from my previous estimate of $375 million, partly because of the first half’s miss. Further, my outlook has changed significantly since I wrote my last report in March. This is mainly because the global tariffs were announced in April, which affected the inflation outlook and consequently consumer expectations.

Net Interest Income to Benefit from Margin Expansion, Positive Loan Growth

Unlike the non-interest income, Coastal Financial’s net interest income has continued to grow in the first half of this year. The company reported a net interest income of $153 million in the first half, up by 20% year-over-year.

Going forward, I’m expecting the net interest income to continue to grow. Firstly, the net interest margin will likely expand amid a falling interest rate environment. The futures market is expecting further 50 basis points Fed funds rate cuts in the remainder of this year, following the 25 basis points rate cut in September. As per the details given in the earnings presentation, around 44% of loans and 69% of liabilities will reprice within three months. As more liabilities than loans will reprice in the short term, the margin will likely benefit from rate cuts this year. I’m expecting the margin to grow by 10 basis points in the second half of 2025.

Another reason why I think the net interest income will continue to grow is that I’m expecting loan growth to remain positive, albeit low. Coastal Financial holds some credit card and consumer installment loans on its balance sheet as well, apart from the consumer loans it originates to sell under CCBX. The growth of these consumer loans held for investment will likely remain low because of low consumer sentiment and rising unemployment among young people, as I discussed above. However, it will likely not go negative because consumers will still need credit for the most essential large purchases. For example, a financially stressed consumer may not want to buy a second fridge for their basement, but if the one and only fridge in their house breaks down, then they will need to replace it.

Commercial is another important segment for the loan portfolio that Coastal Financial holds on its balance sheet. As per the earnings presentation, commercial real estate (“CRE”) loans make up 26.7%, commercial and industrial (“C&I”) loans make up 10.6%, and multifamily loans make up 10.2% of the loan portfolio, for a total of 47.5%.

Coastal Financial’s community bank, which handles the commercial loans, operates in the state of Washington. The state’s unemployment rate is currently near the same level as last year. This is neutral for commercial loan growth.

However, the wage growth has substantially declined, and the difference between wage growth and inflation has narrowed compared to last year, as shown below. This means that businesses are not doing as well as before, which is bad for the commercial loan growth outlook.

Overall, I’m expecting the loans to grow by 4.8% (annualized) in the second half of 2025 after growing by 4.0% (annualized) in the first half of the year. Further, I’m expecting deposits to grow in tandem with loans. The following table shows my balance sheet estimates.

Based on my balance sheet and net interest margin outlook, I’m expecting a net interest income of $160 million for the second half of 2025, up by 10% year-over-year.

Expecting Provisions for Loan Losses to be Stable

The credit quality of Coastal Financial’s loans was quite problematic last year, as the provision expense was almost half the company’s total revenues. Since then, the provisions expense has declined, as shown below. Although it’s still very high, making up almost 30% of total revenues, the improvement is commendable.

The first half’s provision expense is a lot better than I anticipated in my last report on the company.

I’m not confident about further improvement because of the anticipated economic slowdown. The Fed is expecting GDP growth to slow down to 1.6% in 2025 from 2.8% in 2024. Economic hardship is likely to keep delinquencies elevated. Credit card delinquencies have declined this year, but they’re still quite high when we look at the last ten-year history.

As a result, I’m expecting the provision expense to remain stable at the second quarter’s level for the remainder of the year. For the full year, I’m anticipating a provision expense of $152 million, which is below my previous expectation of $300 million. I’ve reduced my provision expense estimate because of the first half’s performance and also because I’m expecting lower CCBX revenues.

Lowering the EPS Estimate to $3.94

In my last report, which I wrote in March, I gave an EPS estimate of $4.67 for 2025. I will have to reduce my estimate now because:

  1. The first half’s earnings missed my expectations
  2. I’ve slashed my non-interest income estimate.

However, I’m still expecting earnings in 2025 to be higher than the earnings last year. I’m expecting higher net interest income and lower provision expense to drive earnings. Meanwhile, I’m assuming the efficiency ratio will remain near the average for the first half of this year, which was higher than last year’s average.

Overall, I’m expecting the company to report an EPS of $3.94 for 2025, up by 21% year-over-year.

Downgrading to Sell

In my last report that I wrote in March, I adopted a hold rating on Coastal Financial. Contrary to my expectations, the stock price has shot up by 33% since then. Interestingly, the short interest in the stock has also shot up during this time.

To update my valuation, I’m going to use the historical average price-to-earnings (“P/E”) and price-to-tangible book (“P/TB”) multiples given below.

Multiplying the average P/E multiple by my EPS forecast gives a target price of $71.2 for the end of 2025.

Multiplying the average P/TB multiple by my TBVPS forecast gives a target price of $78.9 for the end of 2025.

The average price target comes out to $75.0, which suggests a 32% price downside. Coastal Financial currently does not pay any dividend, so the total expected return is also 32%. In my last report, I gave a target price of $78.0. My new target price is lower because I have slashed my EPS estimate.

In my opinion, the current valuation of Coastal Financial in the market is bloated and unwarranted. Even though I’m expecting earnings to grow, I don’t see why the stock price should be so high. Hence, I’m going to downgrade Coastal Financial to a sell rating from my previous rating of hold.

data.title: SOLZ: A Straight Forward Solana ETF
data.description:
As we approach the potential approval of US-listed spot Solana (SOL-USD) ETFs in mid-October, there are a variety of different ways traders may look to speculate on a positive outcome for the US market. One such product is the Solana ETF (NASDAQ:SOLZ) from Volatility Shares. Unlike most of the other products in the market, SOLZ is a futures-based ETF and has no direct exposure to the underlying asset. In this article, we'll look at the fund details for SOLZ, compare it to alternative products, and assess whether Solana could be an outperforming token in the Digital Asset market at this juncture.

Fund Details

Launched in March, the Solana ETF from Volatility Shares is a futures-based product that allows traders/investors to get exposure to the price of the SOL token through an easy-to-buy ETF wrapper. The fund has over $231 million in AUM which makes it larger than SOL-proxy products that have been in the market for a longer period of time.

Fund Assets $231.7 million
Shares Outstanding 9,070,000
Expense Ratio 1.15%
Fund Inception 03/10/2025

Source: Volatility Shares, as of 9/16/25

One quick note on the expense ratio: the 1.15% figure is currently discounted by Volatility Shares for the first year of trading and the net expense ratio is currently 0.95%. As of market close on September 17th, the fund shares have a net asset value of $25.54 per share and a closing price of $25.55. For all intents and purposes, this fund trades generally at or close to NAV. This is important because, as we'll see in the next section, not all SOL-proxies can claim this.

Peer Comparison

Despite the lack of a spot ETF currently, there are several ways investors and traders can play the Solana price in a traditional brokerage account. For the purpose of comparison, we'll be looking at SOLZ relative to the following:

  • Grayscale Solana Trust (OTCQX:GSOL)
  • Osprey Solana Trust (OTC:OSOL)
  • REX-Osprey SOL+Staking ETF (SSK)

Each of the SOL-based products above offers investors exposure to the asset, but they function slightly differently. This table below shows differences in market cap, fees, and structure:

Solana Exposure Expense Ratio Market Cap Structure Native Yield?
Solana ETF 0.95% $211.83M Futures Fund No
REX-Osprey SOL 1.40% $273.34M C-Corp Yes
Grayscale Solana Trust 2.50% $62.37M Closed end No
Osprey Solana Trust 2.50% $30.33M Closed End No

Sources: Seeking Alpha, fund managers

The big benefit of a fund like REX Shares-Osprey versus the alternative is it offers investors the ability to have exposure to staked-Solana for native yield. This is important because Solana currently has an inflation rate of 4.9% and staking yield rate of 6.8%. None of the funds mentioned offer shareholders the ability to redeem the assets underlying the fund shares. Given this, one would think the REX-Osprey fund has the best total return after fees. Yet, since the inception of that fund on July 1st, it is actually GSOL that is beating the rest of the pack:

Since July 1st, GSOL has had a 69% total return versus a 39% total return for SSK. At 55%, SOLZ has been the closest to the true performance of Solana, by far. The outperformance of GSOL and the underperformance of OSOL are due to the closed end nature of how those products are structured.

The chart above shows the year to date trend in premium/discount to NAV for both funds. Interestingly, they traded at relatively close premiums through the first four months of the year. That changed in May when OSOL started trading at a discount and GSOL continued trending toward a higher premium. The biggest reason why GSOL has outperformed SOLZ is simply because the premium has increased from essentially NAV break-even on July 2nd to a 13% premium as of July 17th. In my view, this makes GSOL a 'sell' relative to funds like SOLZ or OSOL and I've argued that several times in the past.

Solana Still Looks Good

As a digital asset worth exploring, Solana actually looks pretty good from where I sit. Last month, I argued that Solana would be the next big DAT winner and so far I think that thesis is holding up well.

Since my Solana article on August 28th, SOL has appreciated by more than 20%. This is better than Bitcoin (BTC-USD), Ethereum (ETH-USD), and Ripple/XRP (XRP-USD); each of which have larger market caps than SOL.

In the all important stablecoin race, Solana has the third largest supply of stablecoins minted on the blockchain. With the exception of Tron (TRX-USD) which trades a minuscule 7x circulating fees multiple, Solana is the cheapest of the major smart contract chains by circulating P/F with large stablecoin supplies:

At an average circulating P/F multiple of 237x in September, Solana is far cheaper than Ethereum or BNB Chain (BNB-USD) even though it has more daily active addresses, does more daily transactions, and is comparable in fees. To be sure, Ethereum has a larger stablecoin supply and processes more stablecoin transactions. Still, as an altcoin with a favorable setup, I think Solana is in a good position.

Summary

I'm of the view that now is a good time to maintain a long SOL position. I take that view for two reasons: first, Solana is a high throughput, fast, cheap chain that maintains a top 3 stablecoin footprint in the public blockchain space. Second, it's cheap relative to major peers and has a DAT bid that I believe is just getting started.

I wouldn't overweight it against something like Ethereum, but I do think it's a coin to overweight against Bitcoin. I've said for several months that I think Bitcoin's cycle top is either close to current levels or already in. As I see it, major alts like ETH and SOL are good hedges against that thesis. In the event I'm wrong about Bitcoin being at or near its peak, I suspect the large alts will outperform.

If I'm correct that Bitcoin is at or near cycle peak, I think coins with strong utility will weather the storm better than those that are purely speculative. I put Solana in the utility category. As far as playing the Solana bull thesis, I think SOLZ will work perfectly fine until there is a spot ETF in the United States. At that point, I would expect it to underperform. For those who want SOL access through a traditional brokerage, I'm going to initiate SOLZ with a 'buy.'

data.title: Intelligent Development Could Make Spyre Therapeutics Best In Show
data.description:
Spyre Therapeutics (NASDAQ:SYRE) is engaging in the clinical development of multiple antibodies with potential in inflammatory diseases. These antibodies have the potential to be best-in-class, but intelligent trial design and development decisions may mean the company could get away with antibodies that aren't quite best-in-class anyway. This article takes a look at SYRE's trials and financials.

What are these antibodies?

SYRE's antibodies target three validated targets, tumor necrosis factor-like cytokine 1A (TL1A), α4β7 integrin, and interleukin-23 (IL-23).

Anti-TL1A antibodies, like Roche Holding AG's (OTCQX:RHHBY) afimkibart, Merck & Co's (MRK) tulisokibart, and Sanofi (SNY) and Teva Pharmaceutical Industries' (TEVA) duvakitug, have already demonstrated activity in ulcerative colitis (UC) and Crohn's disease (CD), and are in phase 3 trials. Although if subcutaneously dosed, they appear to require dosing every two to four weeks, in some cases with an initial IV infusion.

Takeda Pharmaceutical Company's (TAK) anti-α4β7 integrin antibody, vedolizumab (Entyvio), is already approved for the treatment of UC and CD. It requires two initial IV infusions then subcutaneous dosing every two weeks, or continued IV dosing every eight weeks.

AbbVie's (ABBV) anti-IL-23 antibody, risankizumab (Skyrizi) is approved for the treatment of UC, CD, plaque psoriasis and psoriatic arthritis. In UC and UD, dosing regimes involve initial IV infusions, although subcutaneous dosing every eight weeks is possible following that.

SYRE's antibodies then are SPY001 (anti-α4β7 integrin antibody), SPY002 and SPY072 (two anti-TL1A antibodies for inflammatory bowel disease and rheumatic disease respectively) and SPY003 (anti-IL-23 antibody).

SYRE already has phase 1 pharmacokinetic data for SPY001, SPY002 and SPY072. That data shows a long half-life for those antibodies, which should facilitate maintenance dosing every three months (Q3M) or every six months (Q6M). For example, the SPY001 phase 1 data show a half-life of ~80 days. By comparison, vedolizumab has a half-life of about 25 days.

As for the SPY002, a ~75 day half life was demonstrated, which is over three times what is seen with first-generation anti-TL1A's. At least at the time of interim data from phase 1 work with SPY072, there wasn't quite a long-enough follow-up to provide a half-life, although the pharmacokinetics were said to be comparable to SPY002, and compatible with dosing two to four times a year. It should be noted there are other next-gen anti-TL1A's in development, which also have longer half-lives. For example, Xencor, Inc. (XNCR) believes it can dose its anti-TL1A antibody, XmAb942, every 12 weeks, and has initiated a study of the drug in UC.

For SPY003, SYRE only announced dosing of its first patient in a phase 1 trial in late March, and guided with Q2'25 earnings in August that it was on track to provide interim data in Q4'25.

Fending off the competition

Even though there are other competitor antibodies in development for UC, such as first-gen and next-gen anti-TL1A's, SYRE may have a trial design that gives it an advantage.

SYRE notes that Johnson & Johnson's (JNJ) VEGA study showed that combinations of biologic agents can produce next level efficacy in UC patients.

While VEGA shows a combination of an anti-TNF, with an anti-IL-23, provides impressive efficacy in UC patients, SYRE believes anti-TL1A's and anti-α4β7 integrin antibodies can provide even greater efficacy.

Rather than guessing which combination of SPY001, SPY002 and SPY003, would provide optimal efficacy in UC patients, SYRE intends to try three possible doublets in a clever trial called SKYLINE-UC. The first part of that study (Part A) involves each agent used as monotherapy, and can begin producing results in 2026. SKYLINE-UC initiated in May 2025, with SPY001 already having advanced from phase 1, into Part A of SKYLINE-UC. As for SPY002, it is set to enter Part A in Q3'25, with entry of SPY003 into Part A dependent upon the results produced in phase 1 work (recall interim phase 1 data is expected in Q4'25).

SYRE's current timeline for Part A results from SKYLINE-UC is 2026. Part B, which includes additional monotherapy data, but also three possible combinations of SPY001, SPY002 and SPY003, won't produce results until 2027. Nonetheless, I believe those results might be worth the wait, since the company has a good shot with its trial design of finding a regime, whether it is monotherapy or a particular combination, which provides some of the more impressive response rates in UC.

Further, beyond SKYLINE-UC, SYRE is also running the SKYWAY-RD study of SPY072 in rheumatic diseases (rheumatoid arthritis, psoriatic arthritis and axial spondyloarthritis). SYRE announced the first patient had been dosed in SKYWAY-RD in September, and confirmed a timeline for proof-of-concept data of 2026. That means SYRE will have multiple proof of concept readouts in 2026, possibly from three agents in Part A of SKYLINE and from three different rheumatic diseases in SKYWAY with SPY072.

Financial Overview

SYRE finished Q2'25 with cash, cash equivalents and marketable securities of $526.6M. For Q2'25, SYRE reported; R&D expenses of $40.1M, G&A expenses of $11.8M and a net loss of $36.7M. Net cash used in operating activities was $87.6M in the first six months of 2025. At the current rate of burn then, the company would run out of cash in about three years from the end of Q2'25, or about mid-2028. In agreement with that, SYRE expects its current cash to last into H2'28. Further, the company may be able to raise additional funds using its at-the-market program, under which it has $179.1M worth of potential stock sales remaining as of June 30, 2025.

SYRE's drugs were discovered by Paragon Therapeutics and in-licensed by SYRE, but the milestone payments are quite modest. For example, a $3M milestone is triggered on the first dosing of a patient in phase 2.

Thus far we have exercised the Option and entered into license agreements with respect to SPY001, SPY002, SPY072 and SPY003. Under the terms of each License Agreement, we are obligated to pay Paragon up to $22.0 million based on specific development, regulatory and clinical milestones for the first product under each agreement. As of June 30, 2025, we have incurred a total of $12.0 million of milestone fees out of a total maximum of $66.0 million in potential milestone fees across all License Agreements. ... With respect to the SPY002 and SPY072 License Agreement only, on a product by product basis, we are obligated to pay sublicensing fees of up to approximately $20 million upon the achievement of mostly commercial milestones.

Comments on the Paragon-SYRE license agreements, SYRE 10-Q, August 2025.

SYRE does have to pay royalties on sales to Paragon, although these are not even double-digit royalties. I believe SYRE has set up a pretty favorable deal then, that has gained the company access to what could be best-in-class, or near best-in-class antibodies. Given the advantages of the company's combination trials, any slight deficit in performance might be overcome by the efficacy benefit of identifying ideal combination therapies.

The Company will pay Paragon a low single-digit percentage royalty for single antibody products and a mid single-digit percentage royalty for products containing more than one antibody from Paragon.

Comments on the Paragon-SYRE license agreements, SYRE 10-Q, August 2025.

There were 60,400,960 shares outstanding of SYRE's common stock as of July 31, 2025. There was also 13.8M shares worth of Series A preferred stock, and 0.7M shares worth of Series B preferred stock outstanding as of June 30, 2025. The company has a market cap of $930.7M per share.

Risks

There are multiple risks to any long in SYRE, a few of which will be considered in this section.

Firstly, SYRE could report disappointing results from its phase 1 work with SPY003 in Q4'25, which would also mean it might not be able to test combinations containing SPY003 in SKYLINE-UC, meaning a sell off could be particularly pronounced.

Secondly, SYRE might report delays in enrolling Part A of SKYLINE-UC, which would make it less likely they meet their timeline of 2026 for the first proof of concept results. The same reasoning can be applied to SKYWAY-RD, delays in enrollment can hurt the stock.

Lastly, SYRE's competitors can announce compelling data of their own, leading to the stock selling off. Even developments involving therapeutics with differing mechanisms of action in UC and CD could cause a sell off in SYRE's stock.

Conclusions and rating

SYRE has got its hands on several antibodies with multiple validated mechanisms of action, potential best-in-class or near-best-in-class properties (long half-lives) and is planning to try different combinations of them in the SKYLINE-UC study. I find it unlikely SYRE can't come up with a very compelling regime for UC in the next couple of years thanks to this thorough approach. The company has the money to get to a result, including proof-of-concept monotherapy results in 2026, and has shots on goal in rheumatic disease as well thanks to its SKYWAY-RD trial. Given these factors, I currently rate SYRE a buy.

Since SYRE trades above cash currently, and it will burn through a good chunk of its cash as it heads towards results, I stop short of rating it a strong buy, because it could simply pull back just waiting for results.

data.title: DTD: Resilient Income Amid Market Volatility
data.description:
Many dividend ETFs such as the ones I previously covered are focused on delivering high dividends above everything else, and the result is often a bad fund. The WisdomTree U.S. Total Dividend Fund (NYSEARCA:DTD) is taking a different approach. The fund's investment universe is all of the U.S. listed companies paying consistent dividends, and the weights are chosen based on the proportionate share of the aggregate cash dividends each component company is projected to pay in the coming year: not on yields. The result is a resilient fund with strong risk-adjusted metrics, and I rate DTD a buy. The dividend yield is not stellar, but the dividends are still higher than average and very resilient.

In this analysis, I will use the Vanguard Total Stock Market ETF (VTI) as a benchmark for the U.S. listed stock market without the focus on dividend, to really assess if DTD's strategy is creating value for investors.

Dividend Selectivity as a Structural Positive

The market benchmark VTI is using a very traditional market-cap approach to constitute its portfolio. This results in a portfolio of 3524 companies. DTD, on the other hand, is much more focused with 837 companies, mostly "value" ones based on the selection rule that selects companies paying steady dividends, with large size and liquidity. The weighting methodology I detailed in the introduction naturally favors larger companies, but not as much as a strict market-cap approach.

Indeed, despite the smaller amount of holdings, DTD is more diversified than VTI. You can see it clearly if you look at the top 10 holdings in both portfolios: DTD's top 10 accounts for 25.38% of the portfolio against VTI's 32.94%. Details below:

Another striking difference is that the weight repartition in DTD is much more balanced , with no clear focus in top 3 holdings, something you will have with VTI: DTD's diversification level is higher.

If we look at the sectoral split, there are also notable differences linked to the focus on dividends. The most obvious one is the tech concentration, and I think it is a good example of how DTD works, and how different it is from other dividend focused ETFs. The Information Technology sector accounts for 15.51% of the portfolio, less than half of VTI's 36.60%. This is because many tech companies are growth stocks, and those paying dividends are not showing stellar yields. However, as DTD focuses both on size and dividend payment, it still has a significant weight on large tech companies, and given the current market dynamic this is a really good thing: not holding tech is an opportunity cost.

The lower weight in tech translates to higher weights on dividend heavy sectors such as Financials, accounting for 21.31% of the portfolio (vs. 11.50% for VTI) and Consumer Staples, 11.28% of the portfolio against 3.80% for VTI. Overall, DTD's portfolio is value driven while VTI's representative of market dynamics, is more growth driven. Usually, this results in a performance lag for the value driven fund in medium term horizons because since COVID, the market favoured growth companies. You will see below that this is not the case for DTD.

Here is a recap table about sectors:

Sector

DTD (%)

VTI (%)

Financials

21.31

11.50

Information Technology

15.51

36.60

Consumer Staples

11.28

3.80

Health Care

10.93

9.10

Industrials

9.33

12.70

Energy

7.39

3.20

Communication Services

6.32

2.00

Utilities

6.29

2.60

Consumer Discretionary

5.44

14.50

Real Estate

4.56

2.50

Materials

1.64

1.50

DTD is fully passive, but the rebalancing methodology, focusing on dividend expectations rather than past payments, is adding active-like elements in the fund, and this is why the portfolio turnover is high at 17% against 2% for the fully passive VTI. The usual problem with ETFs with high turnover rates is that they cut losers after they became losers, DTD's forward looking approach is avoiding this problem.

Financial Metrics

For an ETF with regular rebalancing, "high" portfolio turnover and niche positioning, I am surprised to see that the expense ratio is only 0.28%. Of course, it is no match for the giant cost efficient 0.03% expense ratio of VTI, but considering the rest of the ETF landscape, this is not a high expense ratio.

The difference in expense ratios is compensated by a difference in dividend yields. DTD is distributing a resilient dividend of 1.92% (not stellar, but very resilient), backed by a 30-day SEC yield of 2.09%. This is 0.78 percentage points above VTI, 0.53 percentage points if we take the difference in expense ratios into account.

Without much surprise, the focus on dividend paying companies, usually more mature than the average listed company, gives lower valuation metrics for DTD's portfolio: a P/E ratio of 21.1 and a P/B ratio of 3.6 against 27.1 and 4.5 for VTI.

This is not necessarily a good point alone, as if you read my previous analysis on dividend focused ETFs, you will find that they always have lower P/Es, but that they strangely are showing higher risk metrics than VTI. This is not the case for DTD. The fund's standard deviation is 14.23% (3 years), 14.56% (5 years), and 14.57% (10 years), consistently lower than VTI's: 15.30% (3 years), 16.40% (5 years), and 15.85% (10 years). The difference is even more striking if we look at both funds' maximum drawdown over the last 5 years: -14.68% for DTD against -24.87% for VTI. DTD's positioning is indeed resulting in a more resilient fund against market downturns.

Now, DTD is indeed less risky, fine, but is the risk-adjusted performance matching this dynamic? Short answer, yes it does. Long answer, let's look at both funds' Sharpe ratios.

For DTD, we have 0.68 (3 years), 0.76 (5 years), and 0.71 (10 years). For VTI, 0.89 (3 years), 0.70 (5 years), and 0.77 (10 years). Both funds are close, as VTI is showing stronger metrics on the short term, DTD on the medium term, and both funds are very close on the long term (even if VTI is slightly ahead). Overall, two different strategies that appear to be optimal. DTD is resilient, showing conservative returns and VTI is more aggressive. In both cases, returns are matching the risks taken.

You will find the actual returns below:

VTI is consistently above DTD, but DTD is also showing strong returns over all time horizons.

The only concern I have about DTD is its liquidity. The fund is 1000x smaller than the giant VTI with $1.4 Bn in AUM. This is not a bad thing and remains significant, but the fund's median bid/ask spread is wide at 0.15%. DTD is meant to be a long term investment, so a tight spread is not as important as with other ETFs, but 0.15% is not liquid enough, and adds to the premium in fees. Monitoring the spread to capture a low level before making a transaction will not be an option with DTD.

Sector Dynamics

DTD is mainly relying on Financials, Consumer Staples, Health Care, and Industrials: defensive sectors with traditionally high dividend yields. The appeal of this focus is dual. The first one is historical, you can see from the section above that it works in reducing risks while keeping appropriate returns. The second is the current economic environment with inflation ticking back up, mitigating the pace of interest rate cuts, but balanced by the slowing job market. Increasing the defensive part of your portfolio can be a good idea, and DTD is a good option to do so.

The high focus in Financials is especially making sense as the industry, especially large cap companies (91% of DTD's portfolio) have benefited from the high rate environment to boost their net interest margins and consolidate their credit health. Lower interest rates will make their balance sheet stronger, but slightly reduce net interest margins. However, interest rates are not expected to return to pre-pandemic levels anytime soon, meaning that the industry's margin will keep being above average.

DTD's positioning is a hedge against tech and growth heavy ETFs like VTI, benefiting from high growth perspectives but also high volatility and stronger exposure to shocks. DTD is focused on sectors with low correlation metrics and stable earnings (such as consumer staples and utilities), making it a good option to complement your VTI position, or a S&P 500 ETF, also heavily focused on large cap growth tech companies.

The defensive aspect of DTD is further strengthened by the 91% large-cap allocation (the rest is 7.5% mid-caps and 1.5% small-caps) meant to build a portfolio of companies with strong balance sheets that can resist market turmoils and geopolitical risks (an attention point is that large-cap companies are often multinational companies with stronger exposure to global trade dynamics, but DTD's focus on resilient companies seems to be mitigating this risk).

You noticed that I keep saying that DTD is a defensive ETF to complement your allocation to other, more aggressive ETFs, and this is because the strategy of the fund will never be able to capture growth from dynamic companies that are not paying dividends and can be a formidable source of returns without additional risk (clear when comparing VTI's risk adjusted returns with those of DTD). DTD can be a standalone investment, but I think the correlation point above and the defensive aspect of the fund will make it even more efficient to boost your risk-adjusted returns paired with a more aggressive growth fund.

Risks

DTD is a defensive fund, but that doesn't mean that it comes without risks.

As it is focused on dividend paying companies, it is dependent on regulations such as dividend tax treatments that both have an impact on your returns and how portfolio companies are valued.

It is also important to note the concentration on key sectors such as Financials or Consumer Staples: these sectors are defensive ones, but they are not exempt from potential sector specific shocks. The regular forward looking screening methodology of the fund does provide a buffer about the companies with stronger exposure to such risks, but navigating a sector wide shock might be an issue.

The key concern for me, as I mentioned before, is the fund's liquidity that is terrible. A 0.15% median bid-ask spread is not acceptable and is the dark spot in DTD's bright profile. When you think DTD, you have to think about bid/ask spread monitoring and accept that you will make your transactions based on the spread of the day.

Focused on large US companies, the fund is moderately exposed to domestic monetary policy and the health of the US economy. The fund's strategy is a clear hedge against such risks, but not an immunity. Interest rate shifts will have effects on the fund's portfolio (less than on the broader market, yes), and this will also introduce some volatility until the environment is stabilized.

Conclusion

DTD is a solid defensive ETF with moderate but very resilient dividend distributions to complement (or replace, if you are especially risk adverse) your total market exposure. On a risk-adjusted basis, DTD is as performant as VTI for a marginal expense ratio problem. It would be a perfect ETF if the liquidity was good, but this is not the case. It is a buy opportunity, which should fit most investors' portfolios.

data.title: Restaurant Brands International: No Visibility On AOI Growth Acceleration
data.description:
Summary

I gave a hold rating to Restaurant Brands International (NYSE:QSR) (TSX:QSR:CA) previously as execution risk and slowing unit growth offset brand progress. There are positives to be encouraged about, but I don't think it's sufficient to justify a buy rating since consolidated unit growth is still weak, Popeyes US is facing intense competition, and BK US plans have yet to translate into unit growth. As such, I reiterate my hold rating.

Strategic Initiatives Gain Traction

Although I am not yet upgrading my rating, I think there are positive developments, which are becoming harder to ignore. The most encouraging data point comes from Tim Hortons, which accounts for ~43% of QSR's adjusted operating income [AOI]. In my previous note, I mentioned that operational improvements and a return to unit growth in Canada were positive signs, and encouragingly, we continue to see this playing out with 2Q25's results validating this-Canadian comparable sales growth accelerated to 3.6% y/y. Importantly, the growth was balanced between check and traffic, with positive sales across all dayparts, including a 5% y/y increase in the crucial morning segment. I believe the improvement is organic because it was driven by well-executed marketing campaigns, including the "Scrambled Eggs Loaded Breakfast Box" in partnership with Ryan Reynolds, the return of the popular item "filled Timbits," and also the launch of a summer cold beverage, including the new "quencher" flavors. All of these are organic drivers that QSR can repeat; we know that they are resonating with consumers not only because they have helped to drive >10% y/y growth in breakfast food sales in 2Q25, but also because guest satisfaction scores have reached their highest levels since tracking of this metric began in 2018.

At Burger King [BK] US, the 'Reclaim the Flame' plan is also showing clearer signs that it can work. While I had previously flagged franchisee buy-in as a key execution risk for the capital-intensive remodels, it appears that I have underestimated franchisees' appetite and the potential uplift in sales from this plan. If we look at the most recent numbers, company-owned stores show that remodels can boost sales, which, from the franchisees' perspective, means more profits, higher return on investments, and quicker payback. The bull case here is that this should drive franchisees to move forward with this plan, which should drive up overall sales in time.

Shifting focus to the international business, which contributes 26% of QSR's AOI, is also showing signs of growth inflection. The focus here is BK China, which was the example I cited previously about franchisee risk. But fast forward to today, 3 months later, I believe the narrative has now completely flipped. The segment delivered strong sales growth of ~10% y/y, with comparable sales up 4.2% y/y, and importantly, was due to the efforts of management. For recap, since taking control in February, management has installed new leadership, refocused marketing, and driven a return to positive same-store sales in 2Q25 (which was a recovery that management had originally expected only later in 2025 or into 2026). This not only de-risks a key growth market but also serves as a strong testament to QSR's ability to fix its business with the right execution.

But execution hurdles and demand headwinds remain an overhang

Despite the encouraging progress, there are still several near-term headwinds, which makes it difficult to turn bullish. The primary headwind, which I have also flagged in my previous write-up, remains the pace of unit development. While management remains committed to its long-term plan of reaching >5% net restaurant growth, I just don't see a path to this happening anytime soon when consolidated net restaurant growth continues to decelerate, coming in at 2.9% y/y in 2Q25. As for the Burger King US system, there is still no evidence to fully convince me that the new plan can drive more unit growth since it also continues to shrink, with a 1.2% y/y decline in its store count. While the international segment's unit saw positive growth, at 5.4% y/y, it has materially slowed from 8.2% y/y in 2Q24. It just seems like the FY28 target for 5% is hard to imagine as of today, and I expect that to limit the upside potential here.

My other concern is the Popeyes brand. Popeyes US, in particular, is still seeing negative comparable sales (saw a 0.9% y/y decline in 2Q25), and I don't expect a turnaround anytime soon since the cost environment is extremely intense, where rising beef prices have driven burger-focused peers to lean heavily into chicken promotions (per the 2Q25 earnings call). While Popeyes has a strong brand equity and a significant international growth runway, the reality today is that the domestic business is facing pressure, and this is another layer that limits growth potential.

Valuation

Put together, I think my view is that QSR is a mixed bag of positives and negatives, and the market is leaning towards negative because the fact of the matter is as of the latest results, comparable sales are still at low single digits, unit growth is not improving, and the fastest-growing brand-Popeyes-is facing intense competition that caused system-wide sales to collapse from >10% to just ~1.5%. You can see that consensus has cut their estimates since the earnings, and that has pushed down the share price and valuation (from ~18x to 16x NTM PE now), I believe. I do appreciate the progress made in Tim Hortons Canada and BK US; they are really encouraging, but until QSR fixes Popeyes and accelerates store growth, I doubt the market will turn positive on this stock.

Conclusion

My rating for QSR is hold. I like the progress with Tim Hortons Canada, BK US, and BK International, but as I said, unit growth remains below the >5% target, and Popeyes is facing huge domestic pressure from competition, which doesn't seem to be going away anytime soon. All in all, I think there is little visibility as to when AOI growth will accelerate, and for that reason, I think it is best to maintain a hold rating on the stock.

data.title: Philadelphia Fed Manufacturing Index: Highest Level Since January
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The Philly Fed's Manufacturing Business Outlook Survey is a monthly survey of about 250 manufacturers in the Third Federal Reserve District, which covers eastern Pennsylvania, southern New Jersey, and Delaware. Participants of the survey indicate the relative level of general business conditions in the region.

Since this is a diffusion index, negative readings indicate contraction, positive ones indicate expansion. While it focuses exclusively on business in this district, this regional survey gives a reliable direction of the broader Chicago Fed's National Activity Index.

The latest Philadelphia Fed manufacturing index showed activity picked up in September, reaching its highest level since January. The index rose 23.5 points to 23.2 this month. The latest reading was better than the forecast of 1.7.

Here is the summary from the survey:

Manufacturing activity in the region expanded overall, according to the firms responding to the September Manufacturing Business Outlook Survey. The survey’s indicators for current general activity, new orders, and shipments all rose, with the former two returning to positive territory. The employment index remained mostly unchanged and continued to reflect overall increases in employment. Both price indexes moderated but remain elevated. The survey’s future indicators suggest widespread expectations for growth over the next six months.

The first chart below gives us a look at this diffusion index since 2000, which shows us how it has behaved in proximity to the three 21st century recessions. The green dots show the indicator itself, which is quite noisy. Therefore, we've included the three-month moving average (purple line), which is more useful as an indicator of coincident economic activity.

We can see longer and deeper periods of contraction during each of the recessions, with shallower contractions in '02, '03, '11, '12, '13, and '15. More recently, the index's 3-month moving average contracted from July 2022 to December 2023; however, no recession was called during that time.

In the next chart, we see the complete series, which dates from May 1960. For proof of the high volatility of the headline indicator, note that the average absolute monthly change across this data series is 8.2.

The next chart is an overlay of the General Activity Index and the Future General Activity Index — the outlook six months ahead.

Firms Continue to Expect Growth

The diffusion index for future general activity rose 7 points to 31.5 in September (see Chart 1), its highest reading since May. The share of firms expecting increases in activity over the next six months (52 percent) exceeded the share expecting decreases (21 percent); 27 percent expect no change. The future new orders index edged up 3 points to 42.4, while the shipments index fell 9 points to 31.0. Expectations for employment were more widespread as the future employment index rose from 12.7 to 23.7. The future capital expenditures index fell 26 points to 12.5.

For comparison, here is the latest ISM Manufacturing survey.

Let's compare all five regional manufacturing indicators. Here is a three-month moving average overlay of each since 2004 (for those with data).

Here is the same chart including the average of the five. Readers will notice the range in expansion and contraction between all regions.

ETFs associated with industrials and manufacturing include the First Trust Industrials/Producer Durables AlphaDEX Fund (FXR), Industrial Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLI), Vanguard Industrials ETF (VIS), and iShares U.S. Industrials ETF (IYJ).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
